name: Agentic Task Dev Workflow
description: Use this to implement a software feature or task. Follows a plan-then-execute approach with architecture decisions, invariant tracking, and final verification.
about:
## Agentic Coding Task Workflow

This workflow structures the full lifecycle of a software implementation task: from understanding and classifying the work, through architecture decisions and incremental implementation, to final verification and handoff.

### What it does

The workflow guides an AI agent through a disciplined plan-then-execute process. It begins by analyzing the task to determine complexity, risk, and the right level of rigor (QUICK, STANDARD, or THOROUGH). For non-trivial tasks, it then gathers codebase context, surfaces invariants and non-goals, generates competing design candidates, and selects an approach before writing a single line of code. Implementation proceeds slice by slice, with built-in verification gates after each slice. A final integration verification pass confirms acceptance criteria are met before handoff.

### Upstream context (Phase 0.5)

Phase 0.5 looks for any upstream document that has already defined what to build -- a Shape Up pitch, PRD, BRD, RFC, design doc, user story with acceptance criteria, Jira epic, or equivalent. The agent uses whatever tools are available (repo search, WebFetch, Confluence/Notion/Glean MCPs, Memory MCP) to find it. If found, two flags are set: `upstreamSpecDetected` (something exists) and `solutionFixed` (whether the document commits to a specific technical direction). When `solutionFixed = true`, design ideation phases (1a-1c) are skipped and Phase 1d translates the upstream constraints directly into an engineering approach. When `solutionFixed = false`, design ideation runs normally but is constrained by whatever the upstream document does specify. The plan audit (Phase 4) checks for drift against `upstreamBoundaries` whenever an upstream document was found.

### When to use it

Use this workflow whenever you are implementing a feature, fixing a non-trivial bug, or making an architectural change in a real codebase. It is especially valuable when:
- The task touches multiple files or systems
- There is meaningful risk of regressions or invariant violations
- You want the agent to surface trade-offs and commit to a reasoned design decision rather than guessing
- You need a resumable, auditable record of what was decided and why

For quick one-liner fixes or very small changes, the workflow includes a fast path that skips heavyweight planning.

### What it produces

- An `implementation_plan.md` artifact covering the selected approach, vertical slices, test design, and philosophy alignment
- A `spec.md` for large or high-risk tasks, capturing observable behavior and acceptance criteria
- Step-level notes in WorkRail that serve as a durable execution log
- A PR-ready handoff summary with acceptance criteria status, invariant proofs, and follow-up tickets

### How to get good results

- Provide a clear task description and at least partial acceptance criteria before starting
- If you have coding philosophy or project conventions configured in session rules or Memory MCP, the workflow will apply them automatically as a design lens
- Let the workflow classify complexity and rigor itself; override only if the classification is clearly wrong
- For large or high-risk tasks, review the architecture decision step before implementation begins
examples: Implement JWT refresh token rotation in the auth service
Fix the race condition in the cache invalidation path when concurrent writes occur
Refactor the payment flow to use a Result type instead of throwing exceptions
Add pagination support to the messaging inbox API endpoint

assessments.purpose: The selected design approach is committed with rationale. No unresolved ambiguity remains about what to build.

dimensions.purpose: Design decision is made, tradeoffs are recorded, and there is no remaining ambiguity about the chosen approach.

assessments.purpose: A deliberate scan for unconsidered alternatives, unhandled edge cases, or untracked risks has been completed.

dimensions.purpose: Active scan completed: either no material gaps were found, or any found were addressed or explicitly filed.

assessments.purpose: Every slice has a defined scope and verifiable acceptance criterion. No slice is vague or open-ended.

dimensions.purpose: Slices have clear boundaries and acceptance criteria. The agent knows what done looks like for each.

assessments.purpose: Invariants and non-goals are explicit enough to verify against during and after implementation.

dimensions.purpose: Named invariants are checkable in the implementation. Non-goals are stated and will prevent scope creep.

assessments.purpose: A deliberate scan for missing slices, untracked risks, or acceptance criteria mismatches has been completed.

dimensions.purpose: Active scan completed: either no material gaps were found, or any found were addressed or explicitly filed.

assessments.purpose: The implementation compiles and passes all relevant tests.

dimensions.purpose: Build succeeds and tests pass. No compilation errors or failing assertions.

assessments.purpose: Invariants identified during planning still hold in the implemented code.

dimensions.purpose: Each named invariant from the plan has been verified in the implementation.

assessments.purpose: A deliberate scan for gaps, issues, or improvements surfaced during implementation has been completed.

dimensions.purpose: Active scan completed: gaps found are either fixed inline, filed as follow-up tickets, or explicitly deferred with rationale.

preconditions: User provides a task description or equivalent objective.
Agent has codebase read access and can run the tools needed for analysis and validation.
A deterministic validation path exists (tests, build, or an explicit verification strategy).
If the task touches critical paths, rollback or containment strategy can be defined.
metaGuidance: DEFAULT BEHAVIOR: self-execute with tools. Only ask the user for business decisions, missing external artifacts, or permissions you cannot resolve.
V2 DURABILITY: use output.notesMarkdown as the primary durable record. Do NOT mirror execution state into CONTEXT.md or any markdown checkpoint file.

ARTIFACT STRATEGY: `implementation_plan.md` drives execution. `spec.md`, when created, is canonical for observable behavior and serves as the verification anchor. Do not create extra artifacts unless they materially improve handoff.

OWNERSHIP & DELEGATION: the main agent owns strategy, decisions, synthesis, and implementation. Delegate only bounded cognitive routines via WorkRail Executor. Never hand off full task ownership or rely on named Builder/Researcher identities.

SUBAGENT SYNTHESIS: treat subagent output as evidence, not conclusions. State your hypothesis before delegating, then interrogate what came back: what was missed, wrong, or new? Say what changed your mind or what you still reject, and why.
PARALLELISM: when reads, audits, or delegations are independent, run them in parallel inside the phase. Parallelize cognition; serialize synthesis and canonical writes.

PHILOSOPHY LENS: apply the user's coding philosophy (from active session rules) as the evaluation lens. Flag violations by principle name, not as generic feedback. If principles conflict, surface the tension explicitly instead of silently choosing.
VALIDATION: prefer static/compile-time safety over runtime checks. Use build, type-checking, and tests as the primary proof of correctness — in that order of reliability.
DRIFT HANDLING: when reality diverges from the plan, update the plan artifact and re-audit deliberately rather than accumulating undocumented drift.
NEVER COMMIT MARKDOWN FILES UNLESS USER EXPLICITLY ASKS.
SLICE DISCIPLINE: Phase 6 is a loop -- implement ONE slice per iteration. Do not implement multiple slices at once. The verification loop exists to catch drift per slice, not retroactively.

steps.title: Phase 0: Understand & Classify
steps.prompt:
Understand this before you touch anything.

Make sure the expected behavior is clear enough to proceed. If it really isn't, ask me only what you can't answer yourself. Don't ask me things you can find with tools.

Then dig through the code. Figure out:
- where this starts and what the call chain looks like
- which files, modules, and functions matter
- what patterns this should follow
- how this repo verifies similar work
- what the real risks, invariants, and non-goals are

Figure out what philosophy to use while doing the work. Prefer, in order: Memory MCP (`mcp_memory_conventions`, `mcp_memory_prefer`, `mcp_memory_recall`), active session/Firebender rules, repo patterns, then me only if those still conflict or aren't enough.

Record where that philosophy lives, not a summary. If the stated rules and repo patterns disagree, capture the conflict.

Once you actually understand the task, classify it:
- `taskComplexity`: Small / Medium / Large
- `riskLevel`: Low / Medium / High
- `rigorMode`: QUICK / STANDARD / THOROUGH
- `automationLevel`: High / Medium / Low
- `prStrategy`: SinglePR / MultiPR

Use this guidance:
- QUICK: small, low-risk, clear path, little ambiguity
- STANDARD: medium scope or moderate risk
- THOROUGH: large scope, architectural uncertainty, or high-risk change

Then force a context-clarity check. Score each from 0-2 and give one sentence of evidence for each score:
- `entryPointClarity`: 0 = clear entry point and call chain, 1 = partial chain with gaps, 2 = still unclear where behavior starts or flows
- `boundaryClarity`: 0 = clear boundary, 1 = likely boundary but some uncertainty, 2 = patch-vs-boundary decision still unclear
- `invariantClarity`: 0 = important invariants are explicit, 1 = some are inferred or uncertain, 2 = important invariants are still unclear
- `verificationClarity`: 0 = clear deterministic verification path, 1 = partial verification path, 2 = verification is still weak or unclear

Use the rubric, not vibes:
- QUICK: do not run the deeper context batch; if the rubric says you're missing too much context, your classification is probably wrong and you should reclassify upward before moving on
- STANDARD: run the deeper context batch if the total score is 3 or more, or if `boundaryClarity`, `invariantClarity`, or `verificationClarity` is 2
- THOROUGH: always run the deeper context batch

The deeper context batch is:
- `routine-context-gathering` with `focus=COMPLETENESS`
- `routine-context-gathering` with `focus=DEPTH`

After the batch, synthesize what changed, what stayed the same, and what is still unknown. If the extra context changes the classification, update it before you leave this step.

Capture:
- `taskComplexity`
- `riskLevel`
- `rigorMode`
- `automationLevel`
- `prStrategy`
- `contextSummary`
- `candidateFiles`
- `invariants`
- `nonGoals`
- `openQuestions` (only real human-decision questions)
- `philosophySources`
- `philosophyConflicts`

steps.title: Phase 0.5: Locate Upstream Context
steps.prompt:
Before you start designing, find out what has already been decided upstream. Use whatever tools you have -- repo search, WebFetch, MCP integrations (Confluence, Notion, Google Docs, Glean, Linear, Jira), Memory MCP, whatever is available.

Look for any document that describes this work: a pitch, PRD, BRD, RFC, design doc, user story with acceptance criteria, epic, Jira ticket, or equivalent. Also scan for URLs in the task description -- if someone linked a document, read it.

If nothing relevant exists, say so and move on. Do not fabricate context.

If you find something, read it and commit to three questions:

1. **Is the problem fixed?** Does this document commit to a specific problem or need -- or is the framing still open?
2. **Is the solution fixed?** Does this document commit to a specific technical direction -- or is that still yours to decide?
3. **What are the hard boundaries?** Extract any explicit effort bounds, out-of-scope exclusions, required constraints, acceptance criteria, or invariants that must hold. Free-form summary -- do not force a schema onto it.

For the two booleans: if you are genuinely uncertain, default `solutionFixed = false`. Running design ideation when it wasn't strictly needed is a smaller cost than skipping it when it was.

Capture:
- `upstreamSpecDetected` (boolean)
- `upstreamSource` (string: where you found it, or 'none')
- `upstreamContext` (string: short summary of what you found and why it's relevant)
- `problemFixed` (boolean)
- `solutionFixed` (boolean)
- `upstreamBoundaries` (string: the hard constraints extracted -- effort bound, out-of-scope items, required invariants, acceptance criteria)

steps.title: Phase 1a: State Hypothesis
steps.prompt:
Before you do design work, tell me your current best guess.

Keep it short:
1. what you think the right approach is
2. what worries you about it
3. what would most likely make it wrong

Capture:
- `initialHypothesis`

steps.title: Phase 1b: Lightweight Design (QUICK)
steps.prompt:
Generate a lightweight design inline. QUICK rigor means the path is clear and risk is low.

Produce two mandatory candidates:
1. The simplest possible change that satisfies acceptance criteria
2. Follow the existing repo pattern for this kind of change

For each candidate:
- One-sentence summary
- Which tensions it resolves and which it accepts
- How it relates to existing repo patterns (follows / adapts / departs)
- Failure mode to watch
- Philosophy fit (name specific principles)

Compare and recommend. If both converge on the same approach, say so honestly.

Write the output to `design-candidates.md` with this structure:
- Problem Understanding (core tensions, what makes it hard)
- Philosophy Constraints (which principles matter for this problem)
- Candidates (each with: summary, tensions resolved/accepted, failure mode, philosophy fit)
- Comparison and Recommendation
- Open Questions (if any remain)

steps.title: Phase 1b: Design Generation (Injected Routine — Tension-Driven Design)
Phase 1c: Challenge and Select
steps.prompt:
Read `design-candidates.md`, compare it to your original guess, and make the call.

Be explicit about three things:
- what the design work confirmed
- what changed your mind
- what you missed the first time

Then pressure-test the leading option:
- what's the strongest case against it?
- what assumption breaks it?

After the challenge batch, say:
- what changed your mind
- what didn't
- which findings you reject and why

Pick the approach yourself. Don't hide behind the artifact. If the simplest thing works, prefer it. If the front-runner stops looking right after challenge, switch.

Capture:
- `selectedApproach` — chosen design with rationale tied to tensions
- `runnerUpApproach` — next-best option and why it lost
- `architectureRationale` — tensions resolved vs accepted
- `pivotTriggers` — conditions under which you'd switch to the runner-up
- `keyRiskToMonitor` — failure mode of the selected approach
- `acceptedTradeoffs`
- `identifiedFailureModes`

promptFragments.text: Run `routine-hypothesis-challenge` on the leading option's failure modes before you decide.
Also run `routine-execution-simulation` on the three most likely failure paths before you decide.

effect.guidance: Address whichever gate scored low: design_soundness low -- the design decision is still ambiguous; commit to an approach and record the rationale before proceeding. design_gaps low -- the gap scan was not completed or found unaddressed gaps; either resolve them or explicitly file them before proceeding.

steps.title: Phase 1d: Spec to Approach (Solution Fixed)
steps.prompt:
An upstream document has fixed the solution direction. Your job is to translate it into a concrete engineering approach, then pressure-test the translation on technical grounds.

From `upstreamContext` and `upstreamBoundaries`, derive:
1. **selectedApproach** -- the technical strategy. Name key interfaces, types, and modules. Do not re-open decisions already fixed by the upstream document.
2. **architectureRationale** -- which upstream constraints drove each technical decision.
3. **pivotTriggers** -- conditions under which the approach needs revisiting (engineering discoveries only, not product pivots).
4. **acceptedTradeoffs** -- engineering tradeoffs accepted to honor upstream constraints.
5. **identifiedFailureModes** -- technical risks in this approach.

Then challenge the translation on purely technical grounds: what is the strongest engineering case against this approach? Which module boundary assumption is most likely wrong? Which integration point is least understood? Record your honest answer before continuing.

If `upstreamBoundaries` includes an effort bound (appetite, deadline, sprint allocation), treat it as a ceiling -- scope down to fit rather than expand. Record any engineering concerns that exceed it as `followUpTickets`.

Capture:
- `selectedApproach`
- `architectureRationale`
- `pivotTriggers`
- `acceptedTradeoffs`
- `identifiedFailureModes`
- `followUpTickets` (initialize if needed)

body.title: Pre-Assess Design Review
body.prompt:
Before the detailed design review, state your current assessment in 2-4 sentences.

Say:
- what you think the strongest part of the selected design is right now
- what you think the weakest part is right now
- which tradeoff or failure mode worries you most

This is your reference point for interpreting the review findings.

Set this key in the next `continue_workflow` call's `context` object:
- `designReviewAssessment`

body.title: Synthesize Design Review Findings
body.prompt:
Read `design-review-findings.md` and turn the review into workflow-owned decisions.

Compare it against `designReviewAssessment`:
- what did the review confirm?
- what did it surface that you missed?
- what changed your mind and what held firm?

If the findings are real, fix the design before you continue (`selectedApproach`, `architectureRationale`, `pivotTriggers`, `acceptedTradeoffs`, `identifiedFailureModes`).

After any extra challenge, synthesize explicitly:
- which findings actually matter
- what changed in the design
- what you reject and why

For any finding that changes the decision, classify it as:
- `Confirmed`: you checked it against primary evidence (code, artifacts, spec, tests/build, or direct workflow context)
- `Plausible`: interesting, but not verified enough to drive the decision yet
- `Rejected`: contradicted by fuller context or direct evidence

Subagent agreement alone is not enough for `Confirmed`.

Capture:
- `designFindings`
- `designRevised`

promptFragments.text: The solution direction was fixed by an upstream document. Do NOT reopen decisions it already resolved. Critique the technical translation only: does the approach faithfully implement what the upstream document specifies? Does it stay within any stated effort bound? Reject any review finding that is actually a product or requirements question -- those belong in the upstream document, not here.

If the review surfaced materially non-empty or surprising findings, run `routine-hypothesis-challenge` on the most serious finding and `routine-execution-simulation` on the most dangerous failure mode before you finalize the revised design.

body.title: Design Review Loop Decision

steps.title: Phase 3: Slice, Plan, and Test Design
steps.prompt:
Turn the decision into a plan someone else could execute without guessing.

**Open questions gate:** check `openQuestions` from Phase 0. If any remain unanswered and would materially affect implementation quality, either resolve them now with tools or record them in the risk register with an explicit decision about how to proceed without them. Do not silently carry unanswered questions into implementation.

Update `implementation_plan.md`.

It should cover:
1. Problem statement
2. Acceptance criteria (mirror `spec.md` if it exists; `spec.md` owns observable behavior)
3. Non-goals
4. Philosophy-driven constraints
5. Invariants
6. Selected approach + rationale + runner-up
7. Vertical slices
8. Work packages only if they actually help
9. Test design
10. Risk register
11. PR packaging strategy
12. Philosophy alignment per slice:
   - [principle] -> [satisfied / tension / violated + 1-line why]

Capture:
- `implementationPlan`
- `slices`
- `testDesign`
- `estimatedPRCount`
- `followUpTickets` (initialize if needed)
- `unresolvedUnknownCount` — count of open questions that would materially affect implementation quality
- `planConfidenceBand` — Low / Medium / High

The plan is the deliverable for this step. Do not implement anything -- not a "quick win", not a file read that bleeds into edits, nothing. Execution begins in Phase 6, one slice at a time. If you find yourself writing code or editing source files right now, stop immediately.

effect.guidance: Address whichever gate scored low: plan_completeness low -- one or more slices lack clear boundaries or verifiable acceptance criteria; sharpen them before implementation begins. invariant_clarity low -- invariants or non-goals are too vague to verify against; make them concrete. plan_gaps low -- the gap scan was not completed or found unaddressed gaps; resolve or file them before proceeding.

steps.title: Phase 3b: Spec (Observable Behavior)
steps.prompt:
Write `spec.md`.

Keep it about what the feature does from the outside, not how you plan to build it.

Include:
1. Feature summary
2. Acceptance criteria
3. Non-goals
4. External API / interface contract if it matters
5. Edge cases and failure modes
6. How each acceptance criterion will be verified

Keep it tight. If something can't be verified, it doesn't belong as an acceptance criterion.

`spec.md` is canonical for observable behavior.

body.title: Audit Plan and Apply Fixes
body.prompt:
Audit the plan and fix it in the same pass. Fix the plan -- do NOT write or edit source code here. If you find something that looks like a trivial code fix, note it in the plan for Phase 6. Do not implement anything.

Look for:
- missing work
- weak assumptions and risks
- invariant gaps
- bad slice boundaries
- philosophy violations or tensions
- regressions from things you already fixed
- mismatches between `implementation_plan.md` and `spec.md` if there is a spec

Before you delegate, say what looks weakest right now and what you trust least.

After the audit batch, synthesize explicitly:
- what multiple auditors agreed on
- what only one auditor raised
- what you reject and why
- what changed in the plan because of the audit

For any finding that changes the plan, classify it as:
- `Confirmed`: you checked it against primary evidence (code, plan/spec artifacts, tests/build, or direct workflow context)
- `Plausible`: interesting, but not verified enough to change the plan yet
- `Rejected`: contradicted by fuller context or direct evidence

Subagent agreement alone is not enough for `Confirmed`.

Then fix the plan immediately:
- update `implementation_plan.md`
- update `spec.md` if acceptance criteria or other observable behavior changed
- update `slices` if the shape changed
- move out-of-scope work into `followUpTickets`
- track resolved findings (cap at 10, drop oldest)

Capture:
- `planFindings`
- `planConfidence`
- `resolvedFindings`
- `followUpTickets`

If the plan drifted, fix the plan. Don't just keep going.

promptFragments.text: Authorship stripping: rewrite the plan's Problem and Selected Approach sections in neutral third-person voice before auditing. 'The plan proposes...', 'The approach implements...'. Evaluate the rewritten version. Also explicitly check against `upstreamBoundaries`: does any slice exceed the stated effort bound? Does any slice violate an out-of-scope exclusion? Does any slice implement something not justified by the upstream document? Does any acceptance criterion from the upstream document lack test coverage in the plan? Flag any such drift as a blocking finding.
Run `routine-plan-analysis`, `routine-hypothesis-challenge`, and `routine-philosophy-alignment` in parallel before you decide whether the plan is good enough.
Run `routine-plan-analysis`, `routine-hypothesis-challenge`, `routine-execution-simulation`, and `routine-philosophy-alignment` in parallel before you decide whether the plan is good enough.

body.title: Loop Exit Decision

steps.title: Phase 5: Small Task Fast Path
steps.prompt:
For Small tasks, fast does not mean shallow. Every item below is required.

**Upstream spec check:** if `upstreamSpecDetected = true` and `taskComplexity = Small`, verify this classification is correct. If the upstream document describes non-trivial scope (multiple requirements, explicit out-of-scope items, acceptance criteria beyond a single behavior), the Small classification is likely wrong. Reclassify upward to Medium before proceeding -- do not silently discard upstream constraints by staying on the fast path.

**1. Confirm all wiring points with tools.**
Don't assume a file you create is reachable. Check every public entry point:
- Does the new symbol need to be exported from an index file?
- Does it need to be imported and registered somewhere (CLI command map, router, DI container, plugin registry)?
- Is there a test file that needs to reference it?
Trace the full call path from the public interface down to your new code before writing anything.

**2. Implement the smallest correct change.**
Change exactly what needs changing. No drive-by refactors, no extra abstractions.

**3. Verify end-to-end.**
- Run build and tests. Both must pass.
- Manually trace the new behavior through the public entry point (e.g. run the CLI command, check the export resolves, hit the endpoint). If you can't do this deterministically with tools, say why.
- Apply the user's coding philosophy as the review lens. Flag any violation by principle name.

**4. Produce a handoff note.**
Output a notes artifact containing a JSON fenced block with the following fields.
The daemon reads this block to run `git commit` and `gh pr create` -- write it exactly as shown:

```json
{
  "commitType": "feat",
  "commitScope": "mcp",
  "commitSubject": "imperative mood, max 72 chars total with type(scope): prefix, no period",
  "prTitle": "same as full commit first line",
  "prBody": "markdown with ## Summary (bullets) and ## Test plan (checklist)",
  "followUpTickets": [],
  "filesChanged": ["src/path/to/file.ts", "tests/unit/file.test.ts"]
}
```

Fields:
- `commitType`: feat / fix / chore / refactor / docs / test / perf (pick one)
- `commitScope`: product area only (console / mcp / workflows / engine / schema / docs)
- `commitSubject`: imperative mood, max 72 chars total with type(scope): prefix, no period
- `prTitle`: same as full commit first line
- `prBody`: markdown with ## Summary (bullets) and ## Test plan (checklist)
- `followUpTickets`: list of deferred items, or empty array
- `filesChanged`: list of every file you created or modified (required -- do not omit)

The daemon will use this artifact to run git commit and open the PR. Do not commit or push yourself.

Do not create heavyweight planning artifacts unless risk unexpectedly grows.

body.title: Implement Slice
body.prompt:
Implement the current slice: `{{currentSlice.name}}`.

Before writing a single line of code, declare your scope:
- List the exact files and symbols this slice touches
- Confirm none of them belong to a later slice
- If you have already edited files from this or any other slice in a previous step, stop and report it

Hard scope rule: you may only modify what is described in `{{currentSlice.name}}`. Anything outside that boundary is out of scope for this iteration -- not "do it early", not "while I'm here". If you discover you need to touch something outside this slice to make it compile or integrate, set `unexpectedScopeChange = true` and do the minimum necessary to stay green, then stop.

Implement incrementally. Run tests and build to prove the slice works before advancing.

Track:
- `specialCaseIntroduced` -- did this slice require a new special-case?
- `unplannedAbstractionIntroduced` -- did this slice introduce an abstraction not in the plan?
- `unexpectedScopeChange` -- did this slice touch files outside its planned scope?

Set `verifyNeeded` to true if ANY of:
- `prStrategy = MultiPR`
- `specialCaseIntroduced = true`
- `unplannedAbstractionIntroduced = true`
- `unexpectedScopeChange = true`
- tests or build failed

Capture: `specialCaseIntroduced`, `unplannedAbstractionIntroduced`, `unexpectedScopeChange`, `verifyNeeded`

body.title: Verify Slice
body.prompt:
Take a fresh look at what you just changed.

Check whether:
- it matches the plan's intent, not just the letter
- it hides assumptions or skips edge cases
- invariants still hold
- it regressed against the user's philosophy
- multiple unverified slices now need to be reviewed together
- `unexpectedScopeChange` was just harmless integration work or real plan drift

If any of `specialCaseIntroduced`, `unplannedAbstractionIntroduced`, or `unexpectedScopeChange` is true, or if tests/build were shaky, run the verification batch before you decide this slice is done.

After the verification batch, synthesize explicitly:
- what multiple reviewers agreed on
- what only one reviewer raised
- what you reject and why
- whether the drift was harmless integration work or real plan drift

For any finding that changes whether this slice is accepted, classify it as:
- `Confirmed`: you checked it against primary evidence (code, plan/spec artifacts, tests/build, or direct workflow context)
- `Plausible`: interesting, but not verified enough to accept or block the slice yet
- `Rejected`: contradicted by fuller context or direct evidence

Subagent agreement alone is not enough for `Confirmed`.

Say where you're least confident.

If the slice drifted materially, update `implementation_plan.md` and `spec.md` if observable behavior changed. If the drift changed boundaries or makes the current plan unreliable, stop and go back to planning.

If the concerns are serious, stop and go back to planning or ask me. Don't wave this through just because the code exists.

Capture:
- `verificationFindings`
- `verificationFailed`

promptFragments.text: If any slice-risk trigger fired, run `routine-hypothesis-challenge` and `routine-philosophy-alignment` before you decide this slice is done.
If any slice-risk trigger fired, also run `routine-execution-simulation` before you decide this slice is done.
If this slice is verified and ready, stop here and package it for review before you move to the next slice.

steps.title: Phase 7: Final Verification Barrier (Verify, Fix, Re-Verify)

body.title: Synthesize Findings, Fix, and Re-Verify
body.prompt:
Read `final-verification-findings.md` and decide what actually needs fixing.

Don't rubber-stamp it. The verifier is evidence, not the decision.

If `spec.md` exists, use it as the verification anchor and make sure every acceptance criterion is actually met.

This loop is verify, fix, then re-verify. If you fix anything here, the next pass exists to prove the fixes worked.

Synthesize the verification output explicitly:
- what the verifier found
- what you agree with
- what you reject and why
- what changed because of the fixes

For any finding that changes final acceptance, classify it as:
- `Confirmed`: you checked it against primary evidence (code, spec, tests/build, or direct workflow context)
- `Plausible`: interesting, but not verified enough to accept or block final signoff yet
- `Rejected`: contradicted by fuller context or direct evidence

Subagent agreement alone is not enough for `Confirmed`.

Fix what has to be fixed now, rerun the affected verification, and update:
- `implementation_plan.md` if the execution shape changed
- `spec.md` if acceptance criteria, observable behavior, or external contracts changed

Capture:
- `integrationFindings`
- `integrationPassed`
- `regressionDetected`

effect.guidance: Address whichever gate scored low: build_correctness low -- the build or tests are still failing; fix them before this step can complete. invariant_preservation low -- one or more invariants from the plan are violated; fix the implementation. implementation_gaps low -- the gap scan was not completed or found unaddressed gaps; fix them inline, file as follow-up tickets, or explicitly defer with rationale.

body.title: Final Verification Loop Decision
body.prompt:
Decide whether final verification needs another pass or whether we're done.

This loop gets up to two verify/fix passes.
- If verification found real issues and you fixed them, keep going so the fixes get re-verified.
- If the issues are clean or resolved, stop.
- If you've hit the limit, stop and record what remains.

When you stop, include:
- acceptance criteria status
- invariant status
- test/build summary
- follow-up tickets
- any philosophy tensions you accepted on purpose

**Handoff block (required for daemon auto-commit):**
Include a JSON fenced block in your notes. The daemon reads this to run `git commit` and `gh pr create`:

```json
{
  "commitType": "feat",
  "commitScope": "mcp",
  "commitSubject": "imperative mood, max 72 chars total with type(scope): prefix, no period",
  "prTitle": "same as full commit first line",
  "prBody": "markdown with ## Summary (bullets) and ## Test plan (checklist)",
  "followUpTickets": [],
  "filesChanged": ["src/path/to/file.ts", "tests/unit/file.test.ts"]
}
```

Fields: `commitType` (feat/fix/chore/refactor/docs/test/perf), `commitScope` (product area only: console/mcp/workflows/engine/schema/docs), `commitSubject` (imperative, <=72 chars including prefix, no period), `prTitle` (same as commit first line), `prBody` (markdown), `followUpTickets` (array), `filesChanged` (required -- every file created or modified).

Then emit the required loop-control artifact in this shape (`decision` must be `continue` or `stop`):
```json
{
  "artifacts": [{
    "kind": "wr.loop_control",
    "decision": "continue"
  }]
}
```
